lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Thus comes the English with full power upon us;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And more than carefully it us concerns

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To answer royally in our defences.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Therefore the Dukes of Berri and of Brittagne,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Of Brabant and of Orleans, shall make forth,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And you, Prince Dauphin, with all swift dispatch,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To line and new repair our towns of war

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: With men of courage and with means defendant;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: For England his approaches makes as fierce

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: As waters to the sucking of a gulf.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: It fits us then to be as provident

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: As fears may teach us out of late examples

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Left by the fatal and neglected English

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Upon our fields.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: My most redoubted father,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: It is most meet we arm us ’gainst the foe;

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: For peace itself should not so dull a kingdom,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Though war nor no known quarrel were in question,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: But that defences, musters, preparations,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Should be maintain’d, assembled and collected,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: As were a war in expectation.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Therefore, I say, ’tis meet we all go forth

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: To view the sick and feeble parts of France:

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: And let us do it with no show of fear;

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: No, with no more than if we heard that England

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Were busied with a Whitsun morris-dance:

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: For, my good liege, she is so idly king’d,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Her sceptre so fantastically borne

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: By a vain, giddy, shallow, humorous youth,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: That fear attends her not.

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: O peace, Prince Dauphin!

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: You are too much mistaken in this king:

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Question your grace the late ambassadors,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: With what great state he heard their embassy,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: How well supplied with noble counsellors,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: How modest in exception, and withal

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: How terrible in constant resolution,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: And you shall find his vanities forespent

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Were but the outside of the Roman Brutus,

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: Covering discretion with a coat of folly;

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: As gardeners do with ordure hide those roots

lines.speaker: CONSTABLE
lines.text: That shall first spring and be most delicate.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Well, ’tis not so, my lord high constable;

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: But though we think it so, it is no matter:

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: In cases of defence ’tis best to weigh

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: The enemy more mighty than he seems:

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: So the proportions of defence are fill’d;

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Which of a weak and niggardly projection

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Doth, like a miser, spoil his coat with scanting

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: A little cloth.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Think we King Harry strong;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And, princes, look you strongly arm to meet him.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: The kindred of him hath been flesh’d upon us;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And he is bred out of that bloody strain

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: That haunted us in our familiar paths:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Witness our too much memorable shame

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: When Cressy battle fatally was struck,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: And all our princes captiv’d by the hand

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Of that black name, Edward, Black Prince of Wales;

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Whiles that his mountain sire, on mountain standing,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Up in the air, crown’d with the golden sun,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Saw his heroical seed, and smiled to see him,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Mangle the work of nature and deface

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: The patterns that by God and by French fathers

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Had twenty years been made. This is a stem

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Of that victorious stock; and let us fear

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: The native mightiness and fate of him.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Ambassadors from Harry King of England

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Do crave admittance to your majesty.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: We’ll give them present audience. Go, and bring them.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: You see this chase is hotly follow’d, friends.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Turn head, and stop pursuit; for coward dogs

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Most spend their mouths when what they seem to threaten

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Runs far before them. Good my sovereign,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Take up the English short, and let them know

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Of what a monarchy you are the head:

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Self-love, my liege, is not so vile a sin

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: As self-neglecting.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: From our brother England?

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: From him; and thus he greets your majesty.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He wills you, in the name of God Almighty,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: That you divest yourself, and lay apart

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The borrow’d glories that by gift of heaven,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: By law of nature and of nations, ’long

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: To him and to his heirs; namely, the crown

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And all wide-stretched honours that pertain

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: By custom and the ordinance of times

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Unto the crown of France. That you may know

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: ’Tis no sinister nor no awkward claim,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Pick’d from the wormholes of long-vanish’d days,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Nor from the dust of old oblivion raked,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He sends you this most memorable line,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: In every branch truly demonstrative;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Willing you overlook this pedigree:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And when you find him evenly derived

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: From his most famed of famous ancestors,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Edward the Third, he bids you then resign

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Your crown and kingdom, indirectly held

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: From him the native and true challenger.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Or else what follows?

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Bloody constraint; for if you hide the crown

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Even in your hearts, there will he rake for it:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Therefore in fierce tempest is he coming,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: In thunder and in earthquake, like a Jove,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: That, if requiring fail, he will compel;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And bids you, in the bowels of the Lord,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Deliver up the crown, and to take mercy

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: On the poor souls for whom this hungry war

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Opens his vasty jaws; and on your head

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Turning the widows’ tears, the orphans’ cries,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The dead men’s blood, the pining maidens’ groans,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: For husbands, fathers and betrothed lovers,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: That shall be swallow’d in this controversy.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: This is his claim, his threatening and my message;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Unless the Dauphin be in presence here,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: To whom expressly I bring greeting too.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: For us, we will consider of this further:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Tomorrow shall you bear our full intent

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Back to our brother of England.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: For the Dauphin,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: I stand here for him: what to him from England?

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Scorn and defiance: slight regard, contempt,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And anything that may not misbecome

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: The mighty sender, doth he prize you at.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Thus says my king; an if your father’s highness

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Do not, in grant of all demands at large,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Sweeten the bitter mock you sent his majesty,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He’ll call you to so hot an answer of it,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: That caves and womby vaultages of France

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Shall chide your trespass and return your mock

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: In second accent of his ordinance.

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Say, if my father render fair return,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: It is against my will; for I desire

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: Nothing but odds with England: to that end,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: As matching to his youth and vanity,

lines.speaker: DAUPHIN
lines.text: I did present him with the Paris balls.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: He’ll make your Paris Louvre shake for it,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Were it the mistress-court of mighty Europe:

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And, be assured, you’ll find a difference,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: As we his subjects have in wonder found,

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Between the promise of his greener days

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: And these he masters now: now he weighs time

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Even to the utmost grain: that you shall read

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: In your own losses, if he stay in France.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Tomorrow shall you know our mind at full.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Dispatch us with all speed, lest that our king

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Come here himself to question our delay;

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: For he is footed in this land already.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: You shall be soon dispatch’d with fair conditions:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: A night is but small breath and little pause

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To answer matters of this consequence.

stage_directions.text: Flourish. Enter the French King, the Dauphin, the Dukes of Berri and Brittagne, the Constable, and others.
Reenter Lords, with Exeter and train.

glosses.phrase: Dukes of Berri and of Brittagne
glosses.definition: Jean Duke of Berry and Jean Duke of Brittany — senior French royals commanded to muster
the Dukes of Brabant and Orléans — more French peers
reinforce and refurbish — fortify
defensive supplies and provisions
the pull of a whirlpool — England draws men to war as a gulf sucks water
foresighted
deadly English, ignored at our peril
deeply respected; feared
fitting; appropriate
assemblies of troops; roll-calls
folk-dance performed at Whitsuntide — the Dauphin reduces England to village dancing
badly governed; ruled by an idle king — the Dauphin's insult
a young man ruled by his moods; volatile
the recently returned ambassadors who carried the tennis-balls insult
restrained when taking offence
Lucius Junius Brutus — who feigned stupidity to mask the wit that would overthrow the Tarquins
manure; excrement used as fertilizer
the Constable of France — supreme commander of the French army
stingy planning

glosses.phrase: miser, spoil his coat with scanting
glosses.definition: a miser ruins his own coat by scrimping on the cloth
has tasted our blood — 'flesh'd' = initiated to meat, as with hunting dogs

glosses.phrase: bred out of that bloody strain
glosses.definition: descended from that bloody stock — Henry's warrior lineage
the Battle of Crécy, 1346 — Edward III's great victory over the French
Edward of Woodstock — hero of Crécy, already invoked in 1.2
Edward III, watching from a hilltop — the French King's image of the Plantagenet king surveying his son's victory
slaughter what nature had made — the French forces cut down by the English
hunting-metaphor: the pursuit is pressing
timid hounds — the Dauphin's insult to those who fear Henry
bay loudly — hunting term for dogs barking
cut the English off curtly
strip yourself of — of the French crown
Henry's claim: the French crown is held on loan, unlawfully
neither an illegitimate nor an irregular claim
dusty, worm-eaten historical records
notable pedigree — a genealogical chart of Edward III's descent
the natural and rightful claimant
violent compulsion — i.e. war

glosses.phrase: thunder and in earthquake, like a Jove
glosses.definition: like Jove (Jupiter) — the Roman king of the gods, wielding thunder
biblical idiom (Philippians 1:8) for 'the tender mercy of Christ'
vast jaws — war personified as a devouring mouth
Henry's answer to the Dauphin personally — a formal challenge
be unbecoming to
cavernous vaults — France's own caves will echo Henry's cannon-fire back
the echo/reply of his artillery — 'ordinance' = ordnance, cannon
the tennis balls the Dauphin sent — named for Paris as their origin
the Louvre — the royal palace at Paris (before it was a museum)
the potential of his earlier, unripe years — the wildness of Prince Hal in Henry IV

glosses.phrase: weighs time / Even to the utmost grain
glosses.definition: reckons time to the smallest measure — Henry now disciplined and precise
already set foot on French soil — Henry's landing is imminent or accomplished

annotations.title: The French divided — two views of Henry
annotations.body: The French council scene stages a revealing internal disagreement. The Dauphin — 'idly king'd' Henry is a 'vain, giddy, shallow, humorous youth' — dismisses the English threat as a Whitsun morris-dance (folk-dance of village England). The Constable (a more serious figure) rebukes him: Henry's reputation for wildness is the 'outside of the Roman Brutus,' a discretion disguised as folly. The Constable's parallel is literary — he invokes Lucius Junius Brutus, the founder of the Roman Republic, who feigned stupidity to mask the wit that would later overthrow the Tarquins. The French court is divided not over strategy but over Henry's character, and the Dauphin is on the wrong side. This prepares us for the Dauphin's eventual humiliation and the Constable's death at Agincourt: the serious reader of Henry is right, and he dies for his accuracy; the reductive reader of Henry is wrong, and he is disgraced.
sources: Gary Taylor (ed.), The Oxford Shakespeare: Henry V (1982)
Joel Altman, 'Vile Participation: The Amplification of Violence in the Theater of Henry V,' Shakespeare Quarterly 42 (1991)

annotations.title: Exeter's delivery — the formal demand
annotations.body: Exeter's speech to the French king frames the English claim in the most aggressive possible diplomatic language: 'divest yourself … of the borrow'd glories … unto the crown of France.' The threat escalates from 'bloody constraint' (line 97) to biblical thunder ('in thunder and in earthquake, like a Jove') to a grotesque inventory of the war's human cost — widows, orphans, maidens' groans for husbands, fathers, and betrothed lovers. The rhetorical work Exeter performs here is to make Henry's claim both total and inevitable. The French king answers with courtly delay ('Tomorrow shall you bear our full intent back'); the Dauphin cannot help himself and provokes Exeter further, earning a sharper secondary threat. The scene closes with Exeter's warning that Henry 'is footed in this land already' — i.e. the invasion is not a threat but an accomplished fact. Diplomacy here is theatre, with the outcome already decided.
sources: Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)
Robert Shaughnessy, Shakespeare in the 21st Century (2012), on diplomatic staging

trials.prompt: What literary parallel does the French Constable use for Henry (lines 36–38)?
options: Lucius Junius Brutus — the Roman who feigned stupidity to mask the wit that would overthrow the Tarquin kings

trials.prompt: The scene stages a disagreement between the Dauphin and the Constable. What does it tell us?
options: That the French court is internally divided on Henry's character, and the Dauphin's dismissive reading is the wrong one — which prefigures his humiliation and the Constable's (accurate-but-fatal) respect
That the French are planning a peace treaty
That they intend to bribe the English army
That the Dauphin plans to flee France

trials.prompt: What does Exeter mean by Henry being 'footed in this land already' (line 143)?
options: Henry is limping from an injury
Henry has already landed in France — the 'diplomatic' demand is actually a formality delivered after the invasion
Henry has arrived at the French court in disguise
Henry has taken up residence in Paris
